content:
4 min read
Just now

--

I didn’t have a full plan. Just some free time during my sem leave, and a vague feeling that I should build myself a portfolio website.
I didn’t know the IDE. I hadn’t tried any web tools before. But whenever I got time, I kept coming back to it — a little bit here, a little bit there — until it slowly became something real.
That was either brave or stupid. Probably both.
The First Problem Was the Template
Every template I found looked the same. Clean, minimal, professional. The kind of thing that looks nice and says absolutely nothing about who built it.
I kept scrolling and nothing felt right. So I stopped and asked myself — who am I, actually?
I’m a cybersecurity student. My world is numbers. Bits. Bytes. Binary logic running underneath everything.
So that became the direction. A background of scattered numbers, bits, and bytes running quietly behind every section of the site — not just on the homepage, but on the skills page, the certifications page, everywhere. A colour palette that felt technical. Something that looked like it was built by someone who thinks in systems, not someone who downloaded a theme.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The homepage — numbers, bits, and a colour palette that felt right for a security mindsetOnce I had that idea, everything else started to fall into place.
Then I Put My Photo on the Page
Normal moment. Profile picture, hero section, looks good.
And then — without reading any tutorial, without anyone telling me to — I right-clicked on my own image.
Save image as.
It downloaded in one second.
Anyone visiting my portfolio could take my photo, my face, my content — just like that. And I’d never know.
That bothered me more than it probably should have. But that’s the thing about studying cybersecurity — you start seeing attack surfaces everywhere, even on your own personal website. I added right-click protection. Then I looked into blocking screenshots on the page too.
Most people building portfolios are thinking about how it looks. I was also thinking about what someone could do to it.
I don’t think I planned that. It just happened naturally.
Free Hosting Was Its Own Research Project
Nobody tells you this clearly when you start — hosting costs money. Domains cost money. Most of the “easy” options have a catch somewhere.
I went looking for something genuinely free, no hidden upgrade wall. Found Netlify. Tested it. It worked. Deployed.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Certifications keep getting added — this page never really feels ‘done.I Still Update It
That’s the part I didn’t expect. I thought launching it would feel like finishing something.
It didn’t. It felt like starting something.
Every new certificate I earn goes on it. Resume updated when something changes. It’s not a project I completed — it’s something I maintain now, like any system worth keeping running.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Skills section — with the bits-and-bytes background animation running behind it, tying back to the whole ‘numbers’ theme.Looking Back, It Was Never About the Portfolio
If I’m honest, the code, the design, the animations — none of that was really the point.
What actually mattered was the mindset I picked up along the way. Studying cybersecurity doesn’t just teach you tools and terms. It teaches you a way of looking at things. You stop seeing a webpage as just a webpage. You start seeing it as something with entry points, weak spots, things worth protecting.
I didn’t set out to build a portfolio “the security way.” I just couldn’t turn that thinking off. That’s what learning security actually gave me — not a checklist, but a lens. And once you have that lens, you start applying it everywhere, even somewhere as personal as your own site.
That’s my honest takeaway from this whole thing — the learning mattered more than the build.
Which makes me wonder — when you built something for the first time with no experience, did the thing you built end up teaching you something you didn’t expect to learn?
Because for me, it wasn’t really about the portfolio.
It was about realising I already think like a security person, even when I’m not trying to.
